summary:
Airway Anatomy & Physiology

A patent (open, unobstructed) airway is the single highest priority in every patient. Without a clear path for air to reach the alveoli, oxygenation and ventilation are impossible and brain injury begins within minutes. The airway is divided into an upper and a lower portion.

Upper Airway

The upper airway warms, humidifies, and filters inhaled air and protects the lower airway from aspiration.

  • Nasopharynx / Oropharynx: Air passages behind the nose and mouth. In an unresponsive supine patient the tongue (attached to the mandible) falls back against the posterior pharynx — the most common cause of airway obstruction.
  • Epiglottis: A leaf-shaped flap that closes over the glottic opening during swallowing, directing food into the esophagus and protecting the trachea.
  • Larynx: Houses the vocal cords (the narrowest point of the adult airway) and marks the transition to the lower airway.

Lower Airway

  • Trachea → divides at the carina into right and left mainstem bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli.
  • The right mainstem bronchus is straighter and more vertical — aspirated objects and over-inserted ET tubes tend to enter the right lung.
  • Alveoli are the site of gas exchange (O₂ in, CO₂ out) across a thin capillary membrane.
FeatureAdultPediatric (key differences)
TongueProportionateProportionally larger → obstructs more easily
Airway diameterWiderNarrower → small swelling causes large resistance
OcciputNeutral when supineLarge → flexes the neck; pad under shoulders to keep neutral
Narrowest pointVocal cords (glottis)Cricoid cartilage (in young children)
Clinical Pearl: In an unresponsive patient, the tongue is the most common airway obstruction. A simple head-tilt/chin-lift (or jaw-thrust if trauma is suspected) lifts the tongue off the posterior pharynx and may be all that is needed to restore a patent airway.

quiz.q: In an unresponsive supine patient, the most common airway obstruction is:
quiz.explanation: The tongue is attached to the mandible; when muscle tone is lost it falls back against the posterior pharynx.

quiz.q: Gas exchange between the lungs and bloodstream occurs in the:
quiz.explanation: Alveoli provide the thin membrane across which O₂ and CO₂ diffuse.

quiz.q: In a young child, the narrowest part of the airway is the:
quiz.explanation: Unlike adults (narrowest at the cords), young children are narrowest at the cricoid cartilage.

flashcards.question: What is the most common cause of airway obstruction in an unresponsive supine patient?
flashcards.answer: The tongue falling back against the posterior pharynx.

flashcards.question: What is the function of the epiglottis?
flashcards.answer: It closes over the glottic opening during swallowing to keep food and liquid out of the trachea and lower airway.

flashcards.question: Where does gas exchange occur in the lungs?
flashcards.answer: At the alveoli, across the thin alveolar-capillary membrane (O₂ in, CO₂ out).

flashcards.question: Why do aspirated objects more often enter the right lung?
flashcards.answer: The right mainstem bronchus is straighter and more vertical than the left.

flashcards.question: What is the narrowest point of the adult airway?
flashcards.answer: The vocal cords (glottic opening). In young children it is the cricoid cartilage.

flashcards.question: How does a child's tongue affect airway management?
flashcards.answer: It is proportionally larger than an adult's, so it obstructs the airway more easily.

flashcards.question: How should a small child be positioned to keep the airway neutral?
flashcards.answer: Place padding under the shoulders to offset the large occiput and prevent neck flexion.

critical.scenario: An unresponsive adult is supine and making loud snoring sounds with each breath. SpO₂ is 90%.
critical.question: What is the most likely cause and your first action?

options.t: Fluid in the airway — suction immediately
options.f: Incorrect. Snoring indicates soft-tissue (tongue) obstruction, not fluid (which gurgles).

options.t: The tongue obstructing the airway — open it with a head-tilt/chin-lift
options.f: Correct. Snoring = the tongue; a manual airway maneuver lifts it off the pharynx.

critical.explanation: Snoring respirations indicate partial obstruction by the tongue. Reposition the airway first; insert an adjunct if needed.
critical.kpi: Recognizes tongue obstruction and performs a manual airway maneuver

critical.scenario: A 2-year-old is unresponsive. A new EMS provider places a folded towel under the head as they would for an adult.
critical.question: What is the problem?

options.t: Nothing — head padding is correct for all ages
options.f: Incorrect. A child's large occiput already flexes the neck.

options.t: Padding under the head flexes the child's neck and can obstruct the airway; pad under the shoulders instead
options.f: Correct. The large occiput means shoulder padding keeps the airway neutral.

critical.explanation: Children have a proportionally large occiput. Padding the head worsens flexion; pad the shoulders to maintain a neutral, open airway.

critical.scenario: After an intubation attempt by a paramedic, breath sounds are heard only over the right chest and the left chest is silent.
critical.question: What is the most likely explanation?

options.t: The tube is in the esophagus
options.f: Incorrect. Esophageal placement produces no breath sounds and gurgling over the stomach.

options.t: The tube was advanced too far into the right mainstem bronchus
options.f: Correct. The right mainstem is straighter; over-insertion ventilates only the right lung.

critical.explanation: Right-sided-only breath sounds suggest right mainstem intubation. The tube should be withdrawn slightly until bilateral sounds return.
critical.kpi: Recognizes right mainstem intubation from unilateral breath sounds
